speaker: interviewer
text: At 95. Stephanie, you were saying that there was a demonstration outside of the British embassy

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Britain declared--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that they would come-- yeah-- that they would come to the aid of Poland. Were there pro-Nazi forces in Poland that were not happy about this particular piece of news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. For example, the Polish students-- [NON-ENGLISH] what-- association-- they had been very, very nationalistic, and of course against Germany. But at the same time, they've been very much against Jews and partly England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so this demonstration took place early 1939, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's took place after the start of the war. The Germans had declared war on Poland and even before they came-- before the news came over the radio that the war was on. We had, already, airplanes bombing us before the official declaration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so this is on-- this is on the 1st of September or before?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 1st of September, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when the planes were coming across. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and at the same time the German had declared war over the air-- over the radio-- that was the first day. And the next day or two, I -- I don't remember exactly anymore-- then England came in. And that's when the demonstration took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, how did your life change on the 1st of September?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 1st of September, we had been back in Warsaw from a summers-- uh, what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer house or-- from holiday, summer holidays. I'd been coming back from college because I'd been doing certification in college. And Vera and her husband and the children and the grandmother they've been in a suburb of Warsaw in a little villa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened on that day. Do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all came back into Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do  you-- what was-- when war was actually declared and Poland was actually at war with Germany what was the talk of what would happen to the Jewish communities? Was there any talk?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, Vera's husband got called up, mobilized. He was a Polish army officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? We haven't mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel. Israel Furst. And he was an officer of the Polish army, so of course he got called up. But he provided for us quite a bit of food. A bag of rice, a bag of flour, a bag of this and that. And not very far from us, around the corner, there was a very big hospital. And after the first few bombings, that hospital got bombed. And they moved over into the Jewish hostel-- the students' hostel-- because that was also a very good and big building. And we had to go. We had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask, were Jewish students still at the university at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very many. No not very many. Because it to was at the very end of the school holidays. There had been very, very few elderly people who sort of lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you had to leave the student hostel were did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into Warsaw proper, to a friend's place. This lady friend, she went to her mother and left us her flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she a Jewish woman or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and do you remember what part of the city that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [NON-ENGLISH] street, [NON-ENGLISH] 20, I think it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the siege-- a few days later because the Germans came into Poland, like into battle. It was a siege of Warsaw. You know that Warsaw has resisted for six weeks. And during those six weeks there had been constant bombings. I'd been in the air defense, and under the roof, when the bomb came down. And the stairs fell down, and I fell up. Anyways, the family was on the first floor, and somehow or another I slipped down. But nobody was anymore in the place which used to be the first floor, and I went into the street and somehow or another I met the rest-- Vera, my older sister, the two twins, and Vera's daughter Nina-- she was only a baby-- a few houses away in the same state. But we missed our grandmother. She must have run into a different direction. And she could hardly speak Polish. She spoke more German than Polish, and at that time to be a German in Poland was not the nicest thing. Anyway, after a few days we found her and brought her back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so where were you living after you were bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few houses from the house which got the bombed until the end of the siege. And then we looked for another accommodation. Vera's husband was away. He was at the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the German's took over the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no water. There was no electricity. There was no food. There would be queues for bread and-- but they started to restore things slowly. And we went into a flat in another street in Warsaw. [NON-ENGLISH] it was called. And after a few weeks Vera's husband came back. He had the opportunity to go across the Romanian border and into the Anders' Army into-- so Romania into Hungary into Italy. But knowing that he had a family with little children and an old woman, he left and came back to Warsaw to look after his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the Germans ordered that the Jewish area be barb wired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, we've been talking about '39. The war and the start to the war, then the siege of Warsaw. That was all '39. And the ghetto was not till '40, and then '41 it was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so what happened when-- I mean for the rest of '39 you were just existing in Warsaw. Did you have work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we started to work, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Israel, or as we called him [NON-ENGLISH], he was a manager of the Jewish hostel and the board of this hostel had a president who was the elder and counselor in the Jewish community-- or Jewish Council, Judenrat, he called him up to the Judenrat and he was made-- he took over the economics department.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel took over the economics-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to work in the Jewish quarantine hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Israel discuss what the Judenrat was thinking at this stage? Who made up the-- who were the people in the Judenrat from the city? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, it was Adam Czerniakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which you know. And you see we brought from college, from my parents' home, very valuable furniture. And when we had to leave the hostel, where the furniture had been, my parents furniture was taken over to Warsaw. The [NON-ENGLISH] sold part of this furniture to the Judenrat because it was elegant, and the Judenrat needed at least one good room where they could meet the representatives from the Germans who called on the Judenrat quite often. Anyway, that helped us too, financially.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who were the members of the Judenrat? What part of the community did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the council. It was the same that was before the war. Don't forget that Warsaw had a very, very large Jewish population before the wall. And thus the Jewish Council was a very active, and very helpful to the community organization. And there had been the president, which was Adam Czerniakow. There was the vice president, which was Mark Lichtenbaum and then there had been councilors, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and this other one I remember, [NON-ENGLISH]. There had been some others as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many councilors were--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the board.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, how many counselors were actually there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there had been up to eight or 10. I don't recall exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the early months of the occupation what was the relationship with the Judenrat and the German officers like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one day they have it decreed that they need to have a count of all the Jews and in 48 hours all Jewish people living in Warsaw had to be registered. So you can imagine what it was like. It was work, 48 hours without pause. And everybody had to come to the council and get registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take part in that registration process?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. And I slept for a couple of hours on one of the desks, because it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- you were registering people who came, yeah. And was there-- at this stage was there any decree to wear-- Star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. No. But it came very soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to wear a white arm band with a blue Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was always or only outside or--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went out onto the street or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In your home, in your house, you could do whatever you wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the information that the Judenrat was receiving about what was going to happen with the Jewish community? Was there any indication that a ghetto was going to be formed or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course there was. It was quite evident that the ghetto was closed, not only formed but closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, but before it happened was there any indication that this was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes, it was quite evident that it was going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- can you tell us about the formation of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From what point of view?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, from your point of view. Where were you? Where did you hear that it was actually going to take place and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was announced through the press and through the radio. Every Jew had to move into a given part, a few streets. That was-- was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can  you-- do you remember that movement? Do you remember that actually event taking place? Can you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot-- I would say most of the Warsaw Jews lived in this area which became the ghetto. There hadn't been very many, like we did, who lived in the Polish side, so to say, among Poles. But the Jewish hostel, the student hostel, that was in Praga, on the other side of the river Vistula. And that was a very Polish part. And we lived all the time in the Polish part.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened with your particular family? Did you move then into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all moved into the ghetto. You had to. You had no choice once you've been registered as being a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what was your accommodation like once you moved into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: First we lived in a little flat in [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we got a better one, bigger one, later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had an apartment--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we always had some-- some flat or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yeah. And what were the general conditions like in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very crowded. Half a million people had been crowded into a few streets. First, there was a larger ghetto, it was so-called the small and the big ghetto. And then when they cut off the small ghetto, in which the original Judenrat, the Jewish Council, was in the [NON-ENGLISH] street. But when they cut this off the Council had to shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain the difference between-- or what was the differentiation between the small and the large ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not really any difference other than streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say cut off, what do you mean cutoff? Can you explain what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some streets, including the one in which the Council stood, had been taken out of there-- declared not to be a ghetto. The Jews had to get out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And move into the so-called larger ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: And move further north to the bigger ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that? Do  you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been end of '40 or beginning of '41, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the-- what was the result of the population into this ghetto area?

speaker: interviewee
text: More crowded, more crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And initially was there still a possibility of going to the non-Jewish part of Warsaw or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Once it was closed, there was none. Once they closed the ghetto there had been gates which had been guarded by German SS people, men, and you couldn't get in or out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from the formation-- but from the formation of the ghetto until it was closed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was some time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was some time-- and in that initial period was it-- was it-- was it possible to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the initial period, it was possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people come from the outside of the city, from-- the Poles come in to do business and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact, some of my friends had been working for them, for the Judenrat. Until it was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their reaction to what was happening with this ghetto? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That depends. There had been different reactions from different people. There had been-- there had been the people who had been friendly and tried to help, and that was the minority. The majority was very happy that the Jews were taken out of the larger city and closed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that initial period you were working at the quarantine hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain to us what the quarantine hospital was and where it was, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the typhus was rife amongst the Jewish population because of the terrific crowding, and those people who survived they still had been carriers. Therefore, they couldn't go back into their homes. They had to come to the quarantine hospital and stay there and recuperate. At least they got some food and proper care for a couple of weeks at least. And then they'd be discharged and could go back to their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that quarantine--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this quarantine was in a former Polish gymnasium school. It was a beautiful library.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] street.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, at any one time, was the number of patients in this hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: 40, 50, around this much. Sometimes more, sometimes less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who supplied the medical facilities and food--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish Council had-- called Hospital and Medical Department. It was Dr. Fliederbaum who was well known to the community. Because before the war Warsaw had a very good Jewish hospital in a part which was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was very eminent. And there was-- there had been other doctors, as well, who came now and again to see what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But economically who supported the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenrat, the council-- Jewish Council.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the council get the funds to support the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had a budget. The Economic Department had budgeting for every other department.

speaker: interviewer
text: But exactly how did the Economic Department raise the money for the department?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same as every other council in the world-- you had to pay some, call it rates or call it whatever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the Jewish community, paying to the Judenrat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only for the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: For everything. And there was no money from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been raided.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, was there any money from the Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the government, from the Poles, not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: There could have been. I don't exclude it. But not that I remember or know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and when did the quarantine hospital cease to exist.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the ghetto was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was what-- around November in '40 when that was? Around that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was, but I don't recall the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a quarantine hospital within the ghetto then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was no quarantine hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is now sort of almost a year after the ghetto was started. What were the conditions like once-- inside the ghetto at the time when the quarantine hospital was closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the quarantine hospital was outside the ghetto, then it wasn't too bad because there was still some movement between the Irish, Polish, and the ghetto side. Then it wasn't too bad conditions. But once it was closed that's when it really became very, very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- for example, once the ghetto was closed, how did food supplies and water and so forth come into that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: The water was city supplied. Electricity was city supplied. The telephones had been still working. And the food was allocated just the same as to the Polish people. You had cards for all sorts of things. And apart from this, smuggling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it distributed? Did it come into the ghetto? Was it sold in shops or ration cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ration cards and some things had been sold in shops as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ration cards were issued--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the council.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the council.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember a funny thing. One Jewish family had a young child, and they kept a goat in their flat for the milk of the child, and they registered this as Mrs. Goat. And they got food cards for Mrs. Goat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there work inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, of course. Everybody had to do something to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and what sort of work-- were there more desirable jobs or less desirable jobs. What were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- you know, Jews are very enterprising. They had their own enterprises. They had all sort of shops and bakeries and this sort of thing. And the Council had employed a big number of people. There had been work squads going out from the ghetto onto the Polish side, especially for building for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about-- were there any going to outside labor factories or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was later. That was in the later stages.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already after Czerniakow's death. You know that Adam Czerniakow, he had committed suicide after getting to know what the plans had been.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you what happened to your job, if the quarantine situation-- hospital-- ceased to exist and you were working there as a secretary. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job in the building department.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to work in the building department. Can you explain to us, please, what the building department actually did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did all sorts of building jobs in and out of the ghetto. Out of the ghetto it was for the Germans. Inside it was ordered by the German Reich. And not-- or this too. For example, they built a beautiful kindergarten, a place with playground equipment. And then they built a bridge across the Polish side of the street. All sorts of jobs. They had been architects, and they had been-- what do you call the engineers who build the roads and bridges?

speaker: interviewer
text: Engineers, civil engineers, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Civil engineers, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and of course I'd been typing all this-- you know, the material necessary. And because I was bilingual, when they had to present it to the Germans-- the plans plus the materials-- I had plenty of work to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the-- when you say you were interacting with the German officers, was this the SS or the Gestapo? Who-- who was-- or just the German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no when you say you were presenting the plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't present the plan. I typed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the typing girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but with the presentation of such plans, what was the sort of relationship now in 1940 with the German high command and the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: There had been two people going to the Germans to present it and sometimes the Germans came to the Judenrat. It was engineer, Lichtenbaum yeah, son of the president. He was an engineer. And then you may have heard about engineer, [NON-ENGLISH]. Probably heard the name [NON-ENGLISH]. Did you also hear about the two painters [NON-ENGLISH]? No? That's another story. Anyway, it was usually the young Lichtenbaum who went to the Germans to present the buildings. Building plans and the types-- what do you call this? I can't remember in English. When you type out all the necessary timber and all the materials for the building?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a tin-- no, no not a tinder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a tinder--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prospectus, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a special name for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other-- how was the internal organization of the ghetto going? There was-- obviously there was all the different departments of the Judenrat, but who were the, for example, who were the Jewish police. The ordinance people? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish police force. Headed by Szerynski who was before the war very high in the Polish police. As a matter of fact, he was in the French Legion. You know what the French Legion was? And he also committed suicide once he got to know what awaits and what was asked from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- sorry. Were they directly answerable to the Judenrat or to the Germans? Who were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to the Judenrat and through the Judenrat to the Germans. And maybe directly, I can't really tell you exactly. Because that was out of my reach. I haven't been the right to know all the details of this relationship between.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the people actually on the gates of the ghetto, who-- was it the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS men.

speaker: interviewer
text: SS men. And in your capacity working in the building department were you able to leave the ghetto and come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a pass and the permission to go into the Polish side without the armband, and I made good use of it. I can assure you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without the armband, why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they'd been embarrassed, the Germans, that a Jew came to their buildings. That was embarrassing for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if you were working for the Judenrat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They must have-- they would assume that you were Jewish, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: They would assume that you were Jewish, obviously, if you were representing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, they knew I was Jewish. But they'd been embarrassed to have a Jewish person coming to their buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was happening with Vera and her husband and the twins and your grandmother, your adopted grandmother, in this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all sorts of things-- different things to different people. We started to plan of course. To live as Poles on the Polish side. Because it was evident that the ghetto would not last forever. And because I had the permission to move without the armband and because I was bilingual in German-- although I rode in the German part of the Polish tramways. And I hired a flat on the Polish side, and we brought out the grandma and the kids, Roman and Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us how you did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember quite. But the children, Roman and Eva, they'd been taken-- and Vera-- they went all together to Otwock, which was a Jewish mental hospital. Which was fairly big. And during the war it got smaller and part off it was turned into a resort, Jewish resort.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is where? Where is Otwock?

speaker: interviewee
text: Otwock is-- I wouldn't say a suburb-- it was a line, a railway line from Warsaw towards the northeast. It was a line which was to later used for going to Treblinka. I think so, it was. Anyway, Vera and all the kids, Vera and Nina, Roman and Eva, they went to the sanatorium, it was called, in Otwock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they get permission to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but how did they get permission to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, that was also part of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not part of the Polish ghetto, but it was the Otwock ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ghetto-- uh-huh. OK. And so you transferred them--

speaker: interviewee
text: You could, you could go. They went by car. Anyway, they went to Otwock. And Ida, the grandmother, she was smuggled out of the ghetto with some furniture on a wagon, horse-drawn wagon with some furniture, which was supposed to go for the Germans in a building. We put her and she was said to be the cleaning lady for when the furniture arrived in the new building. But I'd been waiting already for this wagon in a side street of Warsaw with another horse-drawn wagon and have transferred her from the Jewish one to the Polish one. And taken Ida, together with the furniture, into our new flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so Vera and the children are at the sanatorium, and then what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when Otwock was nearly finished, we got the new that it would be. And we took them out. I provided for them a little house two stations away from Otwock in [NON-ENGLISH]. And the children with Ida stayed there, and Vera, with Nina, went back to the ghetto, to her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they all came to Warsaw? Probably to the flat in Warsaw? Or did they stay in the little house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I told you, they've been-- we've been divided. Ida and the twins they're in [NON-ENGLISH]. Vera and Nina went back to the ghetto to her husband. And I've been floating everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, we'll stop there. Thank you.
